title: Daily Safety Records — The 5-Minute Habit
subtitle: Your conversations are already compliance. Now write them down.
description: You already know safety from experience. This course shows you how your daily conversations map to regulations, and how a simple 5-minute morning meeting — written down — is all the documentation you need.

lessons.title: Legal Protection, Incident Prevention and HSWA Compliance
lessons.content:
## Why Daily Safety Records Are Non-Negotiable in Civil Construction

Daily safety records are not just administrative paperwork — they are a legal shield, a safety tool, and a quality assurance mechanism rolled into one. In civil construction, where hazards change hour by hour as excavations deepen, plant moves, and weather shifts, a disciplined daily recording habit is one of the most powerful risk management tools available.

## The Legal Dimension — HSWA 2015 and Equivalent Laws

**Under the Health and Safety at Work Act 2015 (NZ) — HSWA:**
- PCBUs have a duty to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health and safety of workers
- 'Reasonably practicable' includes maintaining adequate records demonstrating hazard identification, risk assessment, and control implementation
- WorkSafe NZ inspectors can demand production of safety records at any time — without prior notice
- In the event of a serious incident investigation or prosecution, safety records are primary evidence

**Under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (AU — WHS Act):**
- Equivalent duty of care requirements for PCBUs
- SafeWork inspectors have similar powers to demand records
- Records that cannot be produced are treated as evidence that required activities were NOT performed

**The fundamental legal principle:**
*If it isn't recorded, it didn't happen.* In a prosecution or civil claim, an organisation that cannot produce pre-start records, hazard reports, or toolbox talk sign-in sheets faces the presumption that safety activities were not conducted. This can mean the difference between a successful defence and a conviction.

## Records as Evidence of Due Diligence

'Due diligence' is the legal standard for officers (directors, senior managers) under HSWA and the WHS Act. Officers must ensure their PCBU has appropriate resources, processes, and systems for health and safety.

**Daily records demonstrate due diligence by showing:**
- Hazards were systematically identified each day before work commenced
- Workers were informed of daily hazards and controls at toolbox talks
- Plant and equipment were inspected and fit for use before operation
- Corrective actions were identified, assigned, and completed
- Management was informed of safety performance and issues

## Incident Prevention Through Records

Daily records are not only legal documents — they are a powerful tool for preventing incidents:

**Pattern detection:**
When daily hazard reports and near-miss records are reviewed systematically over time, patterns emerge that predict incidents before they occur:
- Repeated reports of the same hazard signal a systemic control failure
- Clusters of near-misses in a specific work area indicate elevated risk requiring attention
- Equipment defects noted in pre-start records that recur signal maintenance program failures

**The Swiss Cheese Model:**
Major incidents rarely have a single cause — they result from multiple failures aligning simultaneously (holes in the 'cheese' layers lining up). Daily records identify single-layer failures before they combine with other failures to produce a major event.

**Near-miss value:**
Heinrich's Triangle (updated as the Safety Pyramid) suggests that for every serious injury, there are many near-misses and hazardous conditions that preceded it. Recording and acting on near-misses — the bottom of the pyramid — prevents the serious injuries at the top.

## What Records Must Be Kept?

| Record Type | Minimum Retention | Reason |
|-------------|-----------------|--------|
| Pre-start inspection forms | Duration of project + 7 years | Evidence of due diligence |
| Toolbox talk sign-in sheets | Duration of project + 7 years | Proof of worker information |
| Hazard identification registers | Duration of project + 7 years | Risk management evidence |
| Incident and near-miss reports | Minimum 7 years (NZ/AU) | Regulatory and legal requirement |
| Corrective action registers | Until closeout + 7 years | Evidence of follow-through |
| Training and competency records | Employment duration + 7 years | Competency due diligence |

## The Business Case for Records

Beyond legal compliance, good safety records deliver business value:
- **Insurance**: Insurers may reduce premiums for organisations with documented safety systems
- **Tendering**: Many clients require evidence of safety management systems including records
- **Worker trust**: Workers who see records acted upon trust the system and report more hazards
- **Continuous improvement**: Records provide the data needed to improve safety performance year on year

## Key Takeaways

- Legal principle: if it isn't recorded, it didn't happen — records are primary legal evidence
- HSWA 2015 / WHS Act: WorkSafe/SafeWork inspectors can demand records at any time without notice
- Due diligence: officers personally liable — records show the system was functioning
- Pattern detection: reviewing records over time reveals systemic failures before major incidents
- Retention: minimum 7 years for most safety records — longer for serious incident records

questions.q: A WorkSafe NZ inspector arrives on site and asks for pre-start inspection records from last month. The supervisor says they conducted pre-starts verbally but has no written records. How will this be treated?
options: Acceptable — verbal pre-starts meet the legal requirement
As evidence that pre-start activities were NOT performed — the 'if it isn't recorded, it didn't happen' principle applies
Acceptable if at least 3 workers can confirm the verbal briefings took place
Only a problem if there was an incident during that period
questions.explanation: 'If it isn't recorded, it didn't happen' is a fundamental legal principle. WorkSafe inspectors and courts treat missing records as evidence that required activities were not performed. Verbal-only pre-starts cannot be verified after the fact.

questions.q: How long must pre-start inspection records typically be retained in New Zealand and Australia?
options: 1 year
3 years
7 years minimum
Until the project ends
questions.explanation: Safety records including pre-start inspections, toolbox talk sign-ins, and hazard registers must be retained for a minimum of 7 years in NZ and AU. This ensures they are available for regulatory investigations, legal proceedings, and compensation claims that can arise years after an event.

questions.q: What does the 'Safety Pyramid' (Heinrich's Triangle) tell us about near-miss reporting?
options: Near-misses are less important than recordable injuries
For every serious injury there are many near-misses below it — recording and acting on near-misses prevents the serious injuries above
Near-misses only matter if two or more workers were involved
Near-misses should be reported only to the insurance company
questions.explanation: The Safety Pyramid shows that serious injuries sit at the top of a much larger base of near-misses and hazardous conditions. Daily recording and acting on near-misses addresses the base of the pyramid, preventing serious injuries from occurring at the top.

questions.q: Which of the following is a BUSINESS benefit of maintaining comprehensive daily safety records?
options: Increased administrative overhead reducing site productivity
Evidence for tender submissions, potential insurance premium reductions, and worker trust in the safety system
Reduced need for site supervision
Elimination of the need for SWMS and permit-to-work systems
questions.explanation: Good safety records deliver business value: many clients require safety management evidence for tenders, insurers may reduce premiums, and workers who see records acted on trust the system and report more hazards — improving safety performance continuously.

lessons.title: Conducting Effective Toolbox Talks, Documentation and Attendance
lessons.content:
## Pre-Start Meetings and Toolbox Talks in Civil Construction

The pre-start meeting (toolbox talk) is the single most important daily safety ritual in civil construction. Held at the beginning of each shift, it is the moment when the day's work is reviewed against the day's hazards — and when every worker has the opportunity to raise concerns before anyone is put at risk.

## What Is a Pre-Start Meeting?

A pre-start meeting (also called toolbox talk, safety huddle, or pre-task briefing) is a structured, brief safety briefing conducted before work commences each day. It is:
- **Daily**: Conducted every working day — not just on Mondays or when inspectors are present
- **Brief**: Typically 5–15 minutes — focused and efficient, not a lengthy lecture
- **Specific**: Addresses the actual work planned for that day — not generic safety platitudes
- **Interactive**: Workers contribute — hazard observations, questions, concerns welcomed
- **Documented**: Sign-in sheet completed — records who attended, date, topics covered, issues raised

## Structure of an Effective Pre-Start Meeting

**1. Review yesterday's work and any unresolved hazards**
- Were all corrective actions from yesterday completed?
- Any outstanding hazards that need follow-up?

**2. Describe today's planned tasks**
- What work is planned? What equipment will be used?
- Any changes from the original programme?

**3. Identify today's specific hazards**
- Weather conditions: rain-softened ground, wind for crane operations, UV/heat exposure
- Ground conditions: has overnight rain affected excavation stability?
- Traffic: new traffic management requirements? Different site access?
- Plant movements: what equipment is operating today and where?
- Utility/service risks: excavation near buried services planned today?

**4. Confirm controls in place**
- SWMS reviewed and signed for any high-risk construction work (HRCW) commencing today
- PPE requirements confirmed for today's specific tasks
- Permits in place (confined space, hot works, excavation near services)

**5. Emergency reminders**
- Assembly point location
- Emergency contact numbers
- First aider on site today

**6. Open floor — worker contributions**
- Any hazard observations from workers?
- Questions or concerns?
- New workers introduced to the team today?

## Documenting the Pre-Start Meeting

Documentation must be completed at every pre-start meeting:

**Pre-start record must capture:**
- Date and time
- Location/project name
- Supervisor/foreman conducting the meeting
- Work planned for the day
- Hazards identified and controls discussed
- Any issues raised by workers — and actions assigned
- Weather conditions
- SWMS reference numbers reviewed
- **Attendance list**: Every worker signs — printed name + signature

**Attendance significance:**
The sign-in sheet proves which workers were present and received the safety briefing. In the event of an incident, the attendance record:
- Confirms the worker was informed of relevant hazards
- Demonstrates the PCBU met its duty to inform workers
- Provides evidence the safety system was functioning

## Common Pre-Start Meeting Failures

| Failure | Impact |
|---------|--------|
| Generic/repetitive content | Workers disengage — poor retention |
| No worker input sought | Hazards workers know about go unidentified |
| Not conducted daily | Shifts start without hazard review |
| Supervisor talks — no questions | Compliance theatre, not genuine safety |
| Sign-in sheet missing or incomplete | Legal protection lost |
| Issues raised but not followed up | Workers lose trust — stop reporting |

## Toolbox Talks vs Pre-Start Meetings

In New Zealand and Australian construction:
- **Pre-start meeting**: Daily — focused on that day's specific work and hazards
- **Toolbox talk**: Often weekly or triggered by a specific topic — deeper dive on a safety theme (e.g. excavation safety, heat stress, manual handling) — also documented with attendance
- Both require documentation and attendance records

## Key Takeaways

- Pre-start meetings: daily, brief (5–15 min), specific to day's work, interactive, documented
- Six elements: review yesterday, plan today, identify hazards, confirm controls, emergency reminders, open floor
- Attendance record: every worker signs — legal proof of information provision
- Failures: generic content, no worker input, missing records — undermine both safety and legal protection
- Toolbox talks: topic-deep-dives — weekly or triggered — also documented with attendance

questions.q: How often should pre-start meetings be conducted on an active civil construction site?
options: Weekly — Monday morning only
When a new SWMS is introduced
Every working day before work commences
Only when new workers join the site
questions.explanation: Pre-start meetings must be conducted every working day before work commences. Hazards on construction sites change daily — ground conditions, weather, plant positions, task sequences — making daily briefings essential. Monday-only briefings leave the rest of the week unprotected.

questions.q: A worker raises a concern about ground stability at the pre-start meeting. What must happen for the meeting to be genuinely effective?
options: The concern is noted verbally but no documentation is needed for verbal items
The concern is documented in the pre-start record with an assigned corrective action and responsible person — and followed up
The concern is escalated to the JHSC at the next monthly meeting
The concern is noted only if the supervisor agrees it is a genuine hazard
questions.explanation: Worker-raised concerns must be documented in the pre-start record, assigned to a responsible person for follow-up, and actually resolved. Documenting without follow-up destroys worker trust. Workers who see their concerns acted on report more — improving safety continuously.

questions.q: Why is the pre-start meeting attendance sign-in sheet legally significant?
options: It determines wage payment for the day
It proves which workers received the safety briefing — demonstrating the PCBU met its duty to inform workers of daily hazards
It is required only for HRCW activities
It is needed only for insurance purposes, not legal compliance
questions.explanation: The sign-in sheet is primary legal evidence that specific workers received the hazard briefing for that day. In an incident investigation, it proves: the PCBU's safety system was operating, workers were informed, and hazards were communicated — all elements of the due diligence defence.

questions.q: What is a key difference between a daily pre-start meeting and a toolbox talk?
options: Pre-starts require attendance records; toolbox talks do not
Pre-starts focus on that day's specific tasks and hazards; toolbox talks are deeper dives on specific safety topics, often weekly or triggered by events
Toolbox talks are conducted by the Safety Officer; pre-starts by the site manager
Toolbox talks replace pre-start meetings on low-risk days
questions.explanation: Pre-starts are daily and task/hazard-specific for that day. Toolbox talks are usually weekly or triggered by a safety topic need — they go deeper on a theme (excavation safety, manual handling, heat stress). Both require documentation and attendance records.

lessons.title: Identifying, Documenting, Corrective Actions and Closeout
lessons.content:
## Hazard Recording in Civil Construction

Identifying a hazard is only the first step — if it is not recorded and acted upon, the hazard persists until someone is hurt. The hazard recording system is the bridge between hazard identification and hazard control.

## What Is a Hazard?

Under HSWA 2015 and the WHS Act, a hazard is a situation or thing that has the potential to harm a person. In civil construction, hazards fall into multiple categories:

| Hazard Category | Civil Construction Examples |
|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Physical** | Unstable excavation walls, unguarded edges, moving plant, overhead lines |
| **Mechanical** | Unguarded rotating equipment, worn tyres on plant, failed rigging |
| **Chemical** | Diesel, concrete alkali, bitumen fumes, asbestos in old structures |
| **Electrical** | Buried services, overhead power lines, portable tool damage |
| **Ergonomic** | Repetitive manual handling, prolonged vibration (whole-body, hand-arm) |
| **Environmental** | Extreme heat, rain-weakened ground, high winds for crane ops |
| **Psychosocial** | Fatigue from excessive hours, workplace bullying, stress |

## The Hazard Recording Process

**Step 1 — Identify**
Hazards can be identified through:
- Daily pre-start site walkthrough — supervisor + worker representative
- Worker reports (hazard cards, verbal reports, digital apps)
- Near-miss investigation
- Regular workplace inspections (weekly/monthly)
- Incident investigation identifying underlying hazards
- SWMS preparation — systematically identifying task hazards

**Step 2 — Document**
Every identified hazard must be entered in the hazard register:
- Date and time identified
- Location (specific — not just 'site')
- Description of hazard (what it is, why it's hazardous)
- Who identified it
- Risk rating: Extreme / High / Medium / Low (Likelihood × Severity)

**Step 3 — Assess**
Assess the risk level to prioritise response:
| Risk Level | Response Time |
|-----------|--------------|
| Extreme | Stop work immediately — control before resuming |
| High | Control within hours — senior management notified |
| Medium | Control within days — documented in register |
| Low | Control within the week — standard procedures |

**Step 4 — Determine Controls (Hierarchy)**
Apply the hierarchy of controls:
1. Eliminate: Can the hazard be removed entirely?
2. Substitute: Replace with something less hazardous?
3. Engineer: Isolation, guarding, physical barriers
4. Administrative: Procedures, permits, supervision, training
5. PPE: Last resort — supplement, not replace, higher controls

**Step 5 — Assign and Record Corrective Actions**
For each identified control:
- **Who**: Named responsible person (not 'site team')
- **What**: Specific action required (not 'fix the hazard')
- **When**: Specific due date — commensurate with risk level
- **Status**: Open / In Progress / Closed

**Step 6 — Verify and Close Out**
The corrective action is not complete until:
- The responsible person confirms the control is implemented
- A supervisor or safety officer verifies the control is effective
- The hazard register is updated to 'Closed' with date and verifier name
- If the control is not effective: reassess and apply a higher-level control

## Hazard Card Systems

Many NZ and AU construction sites use **hazard cards** (also called 'safety observations' or 'hazard slips'):
- Workers carry physical cards or use a mobile app to log hazard observations immediately
- Submitted to the supervisor or safety officer
- Transferred to the hazard register for formal tracking
- Worker who reported receives feedback on action taken

**Hazard card culture:**
A high volume of hazard cards is a POSITIVE safety indicator — it shows workers are engaged and trust the system. Low hazard card submission often indicates under-reporting, not a safer site.

## Near-Miss Recording

Near-misses must be recorded with the same rigour as actual incidents:
- Describe what happened — and what COULD have happened
- Identify root causes (why did this near-miss occur?)
- Implement corrective actions as for any hazard
- Share learnings with other work areas and other projects (safety bulletins)

## Key Takeaways

- Hazard categories: physical, mechanical, chemical, electrical, ergonomic, environmental, psychosocial
- Six-step process: identify → document → assess → control (hierarchy) → assign actions → verify closeout
- Extreme risk: stop work immediately — control before any worker re-enters
- Corrective actions: named person + specific action + due date + status tracking
- High hazard card volume = positive indicator of safety culture — not a problem to be suppressed

questions.q: A supervisor finds an unstable trench wall during the morning site walk. It is rated Extreme risk. What is the correct immediate response?
options: Document it in the hazard register and plan controls for tomorrow
Stop all work in the trench immediately — implement controls before any worker re-enters
Inform workers verbally and let them decide whether to continue
Rate it as High and schedule shoring installation for the end of the day
questions.explanation: Extreme risk requires immediate work stoppage — no worker enters until the hazard is controlled. An unstable trench wall can collapse without warning, creating a potentially fatal confined-space entrapment. The hierarchy of controls (shoring, benching, or sloping) must be implemented before any re-entry.

questions.q: A corrective action is assigned to install handrails on an elevated work platform. When can the hazard be recorded as 'Closed' in the hazard register?
options: When the responsible person confirms they have ordered the materials
When a supervisor or safety officer verifies the handrails are installed and effective — not just that the action was completed
When the due date passes, regardless of completion status
When the worker who identified the hazard says they are satisfied
questions.explanation: Hazard closeout requires independent verification that the control is implemented AND effective. The responsible person completing the task is not sufficient — a supervisor or safety officer must verify effectiveness before the register is updated to 'Closed'.

questions.q: A site has very few hazard cards submitted each month. What does this most likely indicate?
options: The site is exceptionally safe with very few hazards present
Workers are under-reporting — possibly because previous reports were not acted upon or workers fear reprisal
The site is using an effective engineering control eliminating most hazards
The supervisor is conducting thorough daily inspections making worker reports unnecessary
questions.explanation: Low hazard card submissions typically indicate under-reporting — not an absence of hazards. Civil construction sites inherently have numerous daily hazards. Under-reporting often reflects a culture where workers don't trust reports will be acted on, or fear reprisal for raising issues.

questions.q: Which element makes a corrective action assignment effective and legally defensible?
options: Assigned to 'the site team' with an open-ended timeline
Assigned to a named responsible person with a specific action description and a due date commensurate with the risk level
Assigned verbally to the supervisor without documentation
Assigned to the Safety Officer who will decide the timeline
questions.explanation: Effective corrective actions require: a named responsible person (not a generic team), a specific action (not 'fix the hazard'), and a due date based on risk level. Vague assignments fail both operationally (unclear ownership) and legally (no evidence of follow-through).

lessons.title: Electronic Systems, Photo Evidence and Record Retention
lessons.content:
## Digital Safety Records in Modern Civil Construction

The construction industry is rapidly transitioning from paper-based safety records to digital systems. Electronic safety management platforms, mobile apps, and cloud storage are transforming how pre-starts, hazard reports, and corrective actions are recorded, tracked, and audited. Understanding both the opportunities and the obligations of digital record-keeping is essential for modern construction professionals.

## Benefits of Digital Safety Records

| Benefit | Impact |
|---------|--------|
| **Immediate capture** | Pre-starts, hazards, and near-misses recorded at the point of observation — not reconstructed later |
| **Automatic timestamping** | Every entry date-and-time stamped — tamper-evident audit trail |
| **Photo integration** | Hazards photographed in context — far more informative than text descriptions |
| **GPS location tagging** | Records linked to precise location on site map |
| **Real-time visibility** | Managers see records as they are created — no paper chase |
| **Automated reminders** | Overdue corrective actions flagged automatically |
| **Trend analysis** | Dashboard reporting reveals patterns across time, location, and hazard type |
| **Secure cloud storage** | Backups prevent loss — remote access for audits and investigations |

## Common Digital Safety Platforms Used in NZ/AU Construction

- **SaferMe / Incident IQ**: New Zealand-developed — hazard reporting, incident management, dashboards
- **Donesafe**: Cloud-based — widely used in AU/NZ — pre-starts, SWMS, hazard management
- **Safety Champion**: Australian platform — comprehensive HSEQ management
- **Hammertech**: Construction-specific — pre-starts, inductions, plant management
- **Procore Safety**: Integrated with project management — widely used on large infrastructure projects
- **Custom forms on tablets**: Many sites use offline-capable forms synced when connectivity restored

## Photo Evidence — Best Practices

Photographs integrated into safety records significantly enhance their value:

**When to photograph:**
- All hazard reports — photo of the hazard in context
- Pre-start site condition — before work commences
- After corrective action — evidence of completion
- Incident scenes — before any disturbance (if safe to do so)
- Plant condition — significant defects found in pre-start inspections

**Photo documentation requirements:**
- Automatic timestamp and GPS from smartphone camera settings
- Person in photo for scale where relevant
- Multiple angles for complex hazards
- Stored in the safety management system — not just on a personal phone
- Never post incident scene photos on social media — privacy, legal, and operational risks

## Audit Trails — What They Are and Why They Matter

An **audit trail** is a chronological record of all actions taken in a system — who did what, when, and what changed. In digital safety systems:
- Every record creation, edit, and deletion is logged
- Sign-offs and approvals are timestamped with the user identity
- Changes to risk ratings or corrective action status are recorded with the original and new values
- Deletion is typically not possible — records are archived, not destroyed

**Why audit trails matter:**
- In a prosecution or investigation, the entire history of a safety record is available
- Attempted backdating or record manipulation is visible in the audit trail
- Demonstrates the system was not only in place but actively used and maintained

## Record Retention Requirements — Digital and Paper

Digital records must meet the same retention requirements as paper records:
| Record Type | Minimum Retention | Notes |
|-------------|-----------------|-------|
| Pre-start inspection records | 7 years | Also during project |
| Incident/near-miss reports | 7 years minimum | Serious injury: longer |
| SWMS completed and signed | 7 years | High-risk construction work |
| Training and competency records | Employment + 7 years | Certificate expiry tracking |
| Plant pre-start records | 7 years | Equipment maintenance linkage |

**Cloud storage obligations:**
- Data must be stored in a jurisdiction with adequate data protection laws
- Backups must be verifiable — test restoration regularly
- Access controls: only authorised persons can view sensitive records (incident details)
- Data must be exportable in a readable format — vendor lock-in risk must be managed

## Transitioning from Paper to Digital

**Practical implementation steps:**
1. Choose a platform appropriate to project size and budget
2. Train all supervisors and workers on the system before go-live
3. Run paper and digital in parallel for 2–4 weeks to verify completeness
4. Establish offline capability for areas with no signal coverage
5. Designate a data administrator responsible for system integrity
6. Set up automated reporting to management — daily/weekly dashboards
7. Archive paper records for their required retention period — do not destroy prematurely

## Key Takeaways

- Digital systems: immediate capture, automatic timestamps, photo integration, GPS, trend analysis
- Audit trail: complete chronological history — backdating and manipulation are visible
- Photo evidence: timestamp + GPS + in-system storage — never on personal phones only
- Retention: digital records same obligations as paper — minimum 7 years for most safety records
- Transition: train first, run parallel briefly, establish offline capability, set up admin and dashboards

questions.q: What is an audit trail in a digital safety management system?
options: A summary report generated at the end of each month
A chronological, tamper-evident log of every action taken — who did what, when, and what changed — in the system
A list of external audit findings from the certification body
A backup copy of safety records stored offsite
questions.explanation: An audit trail is a chronological record of every action in the system — record creation, edits, status changes, sign-offs — all timestamped with user identity. It is tamper-evident: attempts to backdate or alter records are visible in the audit trail history.

questions.q: A hazard is photographed on a worker's personal smartphone and the photo is sent to the supervisor via WhatsApp. What is the main safety record-keeping problem with this approach?
options: WhatsApp photos are not GPS-tagged
The photo is not stored in the safety management system — it may be lost, deleted, or unavailable for audits and investigations
Personal smartphone photos have insufficient resolution
WhatsApp is not approved for use on construction sites
questions.explanation: Photos stored only on personal phones or informal messaging apps are not part of the formal safety record. They can be deleted, phones lost, or inaccessible when needed for an investigation. Photos must be stored within the safety management system alongside the hazard report they document.

questions.q: A digital safety system vendor announces it is closing down. What is the primary record-keeping risk for a construction company using their platform?
options: Loss of the mobile app interface
Vendor lock-in — inability to export records in a readable format, potentially losing years of safety records
Increased manual data entry requirements
Loss of automated reminder functionality
questions.explanation: Vendor lock-in is a critical risk — if safety records cannot be exported in a standard readable format before the vendor closes, years of safety history may be lost. Organisations must ensure data is exportable, and should regularly export and backup records independently of the vendor platform.

questions.q: When transitioning from paper to digital safety records, what should happen to the existing paper records?
options: Dispose of them immediately to reduce clutter
Archive and retain them for their required retention period (typically 7 years) — do not destroy prematurely
Scan one copy for the office and then shred all originals
Transfer them to the client for their records
questions.explanation: Paper records must be archived and retained for their required retention period (minimum 7 years for most safety records) even after switching to a digital system. Prematurely destroying paper records violates retention obligations and could eliminate evidence needed for future investigations or legal proceedings.
